I think it is better to let user decide whether to run a particular callback synchronously or asynchronously.
If you decide to run it asynchronously, you can use thead, threadpool, coroutine or anything you like to run it. `pymesos` neither have the knowledge of the user's need, nor is able to force user to use any kind of these solution.

And assume, we are now callback in another thread, for example `_io_thread`. you would also be blocked infinitely, because `statusUpdate` cannot be called in `_io_thread` as it is occupied by `reconcile_all_running_tasks`.

If you aware that the old `pymesos` (<0.2.0) is using two threads instead of one, the main concern is we should not make IO lagged by slow callback. it is not for blocking issue. In this new version, we decide to simplify our design for better maintainability. and we see 20% performance boost in our production environment. I think it is worthy.

Install pip (if not installed) - https://pip.pypa.io/en/latest/installing/ 4. Install Cython (if not installed): - Install wheel: `pip install wheel` - Install cython: * Download cython for windows: http://www.lfd.uci.edu/~gohlke/pythonlibs/#cython * `pip install Cython-0.24-cp35-cp35m-win_amd64.whl` (use appropriate filename) 5. Install Microsoft Visual C++ Compiler for Python 2.7: - http://aka.ms/vcpython27

First, do you need to know the knots for some reason? If not, I think the canonical way would be to do something like... 

```python
# Build the design matrix
x = np.arange(100)
dm = patsy.dmatrix('cr(x, df=5)', {'x': x})

# Apply design matrix to new data... 
new_data = np.arange(25, 75)
patsy.dmatrix(dm.design_info, {'x': new_data})
```

If you *really* want to know what the knots where, you could probably dig through the `dm.design_info` object and find it. 

However, it may be a little easier to pull the `CR` class out of the `cr` stateful transform function.

```python
cr = patsy.cr.__patsy_stateful_transform__()
cr.memorize_chunk(x, df=5)
cr.memorize_finish()

cr._all_knots
```

You could also apply to the new data using... 

```python
cr.transform(new_data)
```

- tears.create_information_tear_sheet:  removed group_adjust (brekage)
- tears.create_full_tear_sheet: renamed group_adjust->group_neutral (brekage)
- tears.create_event_returns_tear_sheet:  added optional group_neutral (brekage because it's not the last argument)


Lower level API breakage:
- utils.get_clean_factor_and_forward_returns:  renamed by_group->binning_by_group
- perf.factor_information_coefficient: removed group_adjust
- perf.mean_information_coefficient: removed group_adjust
- perf.factor_returns: rename long_short->demeaned, renamed group_neutral->group_adjust
- perf.factor_alpha_beta: renamed long_short->demeaned, added optional group_adjust
- perf.mean_return_by_quantile: added group_adjust
-perf.average_cumulative_return_by_quantile: added group_adjust, added by_group, changed quantized_factor->factor_data

Ying and I reproduced the results of the Sholl analysis of the axon of the cell OG061123A1-4-IDA.ASC in Check_Sholl_analysis.zip done by @lidakanari at the end of last year. We try to pinpoint the reported differences between Neurolucida (NL) and NeuroM (NM).

The visualization of the Sholl analysis results as given by NL does not help since the figures are very imprecise. 
- They are 2d projections, although what is reported are the numbers of intersections with spheres.
- The resolution of the neurites is very low on these figures. The neurites are much more tortuous than shown (see last figure, also from Neurolucida).
- NL does not give explicitly the coordinates of the center of the spheres (the so-called reference point). Even if its coordinates are computed in the same way like in NM, there can be small numerical differences. The latter in combination with the tortuosity of the neurites can lead to the observed differences between the two applications.

My impression is, that both, NL and NM can be correct and consistent in themselves. The observed differences in the intersections numbers may result from differences in the center coordinates together with the toruosity of the neurites. This cannot be verified though, since the reference (center) point coordinates are not given in NL.

I include a small report and the figures. The Sholl analysis figures start with radius 260 um and 1200 um respectively and proceed in 20 um steps, exactly as the analysis does.
[ShollAnalysis.zip](https://github.com/BlueBrain/NeuroM/files/1041594/ShollAnalysis.zip)

You can fix this with a single python line, I just did it on my system. It's in the gconfhandler.py file, located for me in /usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/guake/gconfhandler.py, just add
proc.stdout = proc.communicate()
after the line
proc = subprocess.Popen(['gsettings', 'get', DCONF_MONOSPACE_FONT_PATH,
                                     DCONF_MONOSPACE_FONT_KEY], stdout=subprocess.PIPE)
if you do it manually on an installed version, you might want to recompile the file :
cd /usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/guake
python -m compileall gconfhandler.py
and 
python -O -m compileall gconfhandler.py

This latest commit aims to better support unicode in Python 2. It is untested on Python 3 so if you get the chance to try it please let me know how it goes, and whether you are still observing the above errors.

The idea here is that we want to:
- Use unicode strings as much as possible (default in Python 3), and encode/decode when packing and unpacking respectively
- Avoid calling `encode()` on a byte string (because Python 2 will implicitly decode it as ascii)
- Avoid concatenating byte and unicode strings (again because of implicit decoding in Python 2)

### Before the change: 30 seconds
![capture d ecran 2018-01-05 a 17 29 00](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1410356/34618123-06be6b38-f23e-11e7-9963-209c4f7a70ab.png)

![capture d ecran 2018-01-05 a 17 26 34](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1410356/34618144-124ce4de-f23e-11e7-95e5-7edd1a43674f.png)

## Context

`dpath.util.set(input_data, path, entity_result)` was very slow on `input_data` which is a relatively big object.

Using a proxy object `results` in my commit, adding results with `dpath.util.new` and then _filling the holes_ in one call _dpath.util.merge(input_data, results)_ is much faster.

The failing test is due to missing positional arguments in the `_depreciated_alias` function in **pyart/exceptions.py**.  The string needs a 0 and 1 added inside the brackets to read:

``` Python
            ("{0} has been depreciated and will be removed in future " +
             "versions of Py-ART, pleases use {1}. ").format(
                old_name, new_name), category=DepreciatedFunctionName)
```
    
Apparently the syntax of Python's .format strings was updated in Python 2.7 to allow the omission of the position arguments but this omission not allowed in Python 2.6 (ie `"{} {}".format("Hello", "World") is valid 2.7 but not 2.6).

@gamaanderson  Addressing your two points:

1. Agreed that we should be able to pass a instance of a Grid or Radar object or any future classes. I would prefer the parameter names **container** and **selection** with some documentation on what these typically represent.

2. Having all the internal field names present in the DAFAULT_* dictionaries is a minimal requirement in order that all default field names have a default colormap and limits.  This will also make these dictionaries play nicely with the simple customization section of the file. The user (maybe even the default configuration) could include additional field names mappings although this could cause conflicts if the default field names are changed. Such addition would be fully supported and recommended.  The preference here would be files have default field names and everything works nicely out of the box, but alternative field names would be supported given their prevalence. 

As for using a intermediate PHYSICAL_QUALTITIES dictionary, I'm against it.  I think it added an unnecessary level of abstraction and complication while saving very little in terms of repetition.   To quote the Zen of Python "Explicit is better than implicit, Simple is better than complex."

First, the answer: While the underlying `SubscriberClient` object you found has a `pull` method, it is not present on the object that is exported as `google.cloud.pubsub_v1.SubscriberClient`. This is an intentional decision made at the behest of the Pub/Sub team, who believes that using the polling `pull` method is generally user error. If you really want to use `pull`, you can do so by means of a slightly longer import:

```python
from google.cloud.pubsub_v1.gapic.subscriber_client import SubscriberClient
client = SubscriberClient()  # This will have a .pull() method.

Sure, the program I'm working on is rather simple. Its purpose is to read objects from our primary database (Postgres), and then write denormalised representations of them to our frontend database (Elasticsearch), which is a process we called indexing. Object UUIDs to be indexed come out of a Pub/Sub queue which is fed by the program which got these objects into Postgres in the first place.

Now, it takes quite a long time (comparatively) to build these Elastic representations but Postgres can sustain a very high read concurrency, so we pump an `asyncio.Queue` and use a configurable number of asynchronous workers to process the objects. This keeps performance up and memory usage down. We do this using `aiopg`, `aioes` and an unreleasable partial Pub/Sub client based on `aiohttp`. The program resembles this:

```python
import asyncio
from .private.indexer import process_to_elastic
from .private.pubsub import Subscription

CONCURRENCY = 8

queue = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=10)


async def work_producer():
    while True:
        messages = Subscription.pull_many()
        for message in messages:
            await queue.put(message.data)


async def index_worker():
    while True:
        work = await queue.get()
        await process_to_elastic(work)


async def begin():
    await asyncio.gather(*((work_producer(),) + (index_worker() for _ in range(CONCURRENCY))))


if __name__ == "__main__":
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    loop.run_until_complete(begin())
    loop.close()
```

As a side note, I think the implementation currently - "we provide a producer that you attach a callback to" - would be suboptimal for us. Our indexing process can lock up for a variety of reasons so we need to be able to apply backpressure to producing messages from the Pub/Sub queue, otherwise memory could grow unbounded and we risk flooding some poor database with millions of requests in the same instant.

I hope this helps! Oh and the in-code documentation has served me fine so far, thanks.

It definitely strikes me as odd that py.test made such a major change (particularly in an incremental release). Additionally, there are warnings that you do not want to fail your build (e.g. `PendingDeprecationWarning`).

That said, I think @dhermes is right on the solution in this case. The `warnings.catch_warnings` API is for catching warnings, yes, but its purpose is for ignoring warnings _in production_, not in your test suite, and not if the thing you are trying to test is whether a warning happened. The `catch_warnings` context manager, for example, will not bomb out if it _does not_ get a warning.

`mock.patch.object(warnings, 'warn')` seems to me to be the correct way to trap a warning _in a test_, in particular because not only do you not want to get the warning, but you _also_ want to assert that the warning was called (e.g. `warn.assert_called_once()`). Your test should fail if the warning did not happen.

So, some context:

Let's say we have an API at v1 and we know one of the methods returns the following:

```python
{
    'field1': 'meep'
}
```

So, let's say they decide this method should return *more* info. They decide to add another field to the return value. This *does not* necessitate a major version bump. The new response is:

```python
{
    'field1': 'meep',
    'field2': 'moop
}
```

Our argument here is that this client library should be robust against this happening. The API should be able to add additional fields without the client blowing up. We don't have the expectation that this extra data will be immediately available without an update, just that it doesn't choke on the response.

There is precedence to this. Compute has kept API v1 for years despite adding new resources, parameters, and response fields. APIs generally only bump versions when they *remove* or *change* an existing thing.

@lupesko yes I have figured that out now. To rephrase my issue (and current understanding) is:
- The `signature.json` file lets you define an `input_type` as `application/json`.
- However MMS expects always input send as `content-type`:`multipart/form-data`. This doesn't appear documented except in the `api-description` endpoint.

What is the issue? I think using the `application/json` value for the input type whilst the actual expected `Content-Type` is `multipart/form-data` is confusing. The value `application/json` is a standard value of the `Content-Type` header, and here it is used to say that format of the `form-data` value is `json`.

In my view, the ideal solution is to allow user to define the `Content-Type` of their request, so that the client can use the much more common `POST` requests with `application/json` rather than using the `form-data` format.

If you want to remain with `form-data` I would suggest changing the `input_type`:`application/json` to `input_type`:`json` to make it clear this isn't about the `Content-Type` of the request, but the encoding of the data passed in the form.

I would also suggest to document the fact that the request is expected to be using `multipart/form-data` in the github repo documentation as well.

It would be good to distinguish between vector graphics and raster graphics in this discussion. Matplotlib has an internal description of lines, text and other shapes that it can export to formats like eps and pdf, giving small file sizes and crisp images even when zoomed in. It can also export to e.g. PNG, in which case a "snapshot" is made at a certain resolution and dpi. (This generally results in a larger file size, unless your plot contains *a lot* of points.)

Imageio is targeted entirely on raster images. You read an image, and get a numpy array that contains the pixels (e.g. NxMx3 for RGB images). Similarly, you can use imageio to save such numpy arrays to a variety of formats.

So when you store an image with imageio to both png and pdf (when we have a plugin for it), the pdf will likely be *larger* than the png, because the PDF will simply embed the raster image bitmap.

The use-case for pfd support in imageio would be to make it easy to e.g. add images to a pdf presentation. And to e.g. read all images contained in a PDF document. In the latter case, its probably only feasable to read the images that are already in raster format, i.e. reading a figure exported by matplotlib will likely not work --- unless we make use of some sort of external program that can "render" the image.

I looked up this travis env variable:

TRAVIS_COMMIT_RANGE: The range of commits that were included in the push or pull request.
TBH, I don't understand well how it's determined which commits are in the commit range, but it appears that it includes more than just the commits listed on the PR. For instance if your branch is behind the target then it will contain subsequent commits as well... which means that potentially it could include commits that have the string [ setup ] in the message even if your PR doesn't include such a commit, AFAICT. For the same reason, if someone changed a file that matches one of the targets then your PR could run the build tests even if you didn't change those files since it will be in the changeset of the commit range.

Not sure if it's possible to work around this. What do you think? Does it even matter? My only thought is that it sort of gives the wrong impression of when the conditional test is run. If in reality we can't control it precisely, then I think we might as well just run such a test all the time. With the sdist in particular, why not build and install from it every time by default?

As you mentioned, the current version of libjuju, [0.4.3](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/juju/), has [a fix](https://pythonhosted.org/juju/#id1) for this issue.  It does also include a fix so that new, backwards compatible field additions don't cause such a break in the future.

The promise that Juju makes is that the facade version will be updated if any non-backwards-compatible change is made, such as a new optional field being added, or new data being returned by the API.  I believe it is also the case that a given major version release of Juju promises to support all older facade versions up to the start of that major version, meaning that a client supporting any version of 2.x should be able to talk to any controller of equal or higher revision, and may be able to talk to a controller of lower revision as long as it is willing to use the older facade versions with appropriate reduction in functionality.

With libjuju, we are targeting the former but not the latter.  Unfortunately, before 0.4.3, libjuju took issue with unexpected new data coming in from the API, but that has now been resolved.  We're also working to get libjuju tests added as part of the review process for Juju core to ensure that libjuju is updated in a timely fashion in response to changes to the Juju API.

> Currently. the wampcc router is sending a PUBLISHED ack message for every PUBLISH it receives. I cannot find anything in the [wamp-proto RFC](https://wamp-proto.org/static/rfc/draft-oberstet-hybi-crossbar-wamp.html#rfc.section.9.2.2) that requires the session to be closed on receiving an unwanted PUBLISHED message. 

Sending an unsolicited PUBLISHED message is a WAMP protocol error, and
conforming WAMP peers MUST close the connection.

Up till recently, the spec required a hard drop of the connection - that
has been relaxed:
https://wamp-proto.org/static/rfc/draft-oberstet-hybi-crossbar-wamp.html#protocol_errors

About the subpixel refinement, I did some comparative tests a long time ago between the Newton method and non iterative center-of-mass. Newton was basically insensitive to pixel locking: the distribution of fractional part of coordinates was always flat whatever the input parameters. (By the way, the 2D distribution in the (X,Y) plane is a better criterion than two 1D distributions along each dimension.) I never compared with least squares fitting that is at least an order of magnitude slower. In terms of speed, Newton required 10 float multiplications, one float division and 9 float additions per particle. Probably slightly slower than center-of-mass but not much once the data is in cache.

About deconvolution, it is literally quick and dirty. A "real" deconvolution software tries to restore a crispy image, but all I wanted was band-passed blobs corrected from the microscope point-spread-function. The later is a much easier and thus quicker task. I need an isotropic sample in the exact same conditions and acquisition parameters to measure a kernel. Then, I apply this kernel on the already band passed image, almost noiseless. Also, the deconvolution is only along Z axis. Since it is a 1D convolution, this process is extremely fast. On a 512^3 image it took a fraction of the time needed to track the particles. You also have the advantage of doing it in the same processing pipeline. Huygens reads the image, deconvolves, writes on disk, then Trackpy reads the deconvolved image and tracks. Here you read once, deconvolve and track in the same breath.

The main blocker for that is the ability to bind to port 80 without being a root user. It's a security issue, and this is why these projects (including postgres) recommend binding to a port higher than 1024 like port 5000 and port 9000, then let projects like `nginx` bind to "standard" ports because they handle permissions correctly by binding to the specified port as root, then drop privileges to the `Nobody` user for the workers accepting incoming connections.

I wasn't really suggesting only doing it for AWS.  CloudFormation was just an example.  Today, the provisioning stuff for all providers uses some common cloud-config that's in contrib/coreos.  My point was that it would be bad to force users to maintain configuration / metadata _separate_ from that.  It adds a lot of complexity and probably won't work well at all for those auto-scaling their data plane.  (Going forward, how would new nodes in an existing cluster get the right metadata?)

Taking a giant step back here, if we want to enable metadata-driven placement options for each plane, such that each plane can optionally be isolated, there are fundamentally two ways to do that without introducing a breaking change:

1. Find a way to update metadata of existing nodes in an existing cluster as well as metadata for future nodes in an existing cluster (owing to the possibility that the data plane will scale; come to think of it, coreos/fleet#1077 might not fully address that case).  If this could be achieved somehow, this would allow a consistent and _static_ set of units to be used that can reference certain, well-known tags like `controlPlane=true` with the assumption such machines will exist somewhere in the cluster, regardless of whether that cluster is new or pre-existing.  I think there are a lot of gotchas that would need to be carefully worked out here.

2. Find a way to conditionally decorate units with appropriate `MachineMetadata` constraints _only_ for new clusters where an operator has explicitly chosen to isolate their planes and has metadata set appropriately to support that.

Do we agree these are the two options?  If so, we can work through the pros and cons of each, because I think there's a lot of complexity hiding in both options.

If you have a running aurora scheduler, http://your-scheduler/apiclient/api.html ought to give you a page explaining all the thrift api endpoints.

As for container types: What the docs actually mean there is the only container type **other than normal mesos containers** is "docker".  If you don't explicitly configure a docker image for an aurora job, it will use the mesos default.

Environment variables: thermos executor handles setting up the task runtime environment.  It actually doesn't have explicit hooks for setting environment variables, which still confuses me.  It does easily let you run arbitrary shell scripts to set up your task, and thermos sources a .thermos_profile file if it is present when starting processes, so you can simply do something like

```sh
cat > .thermos_profile <<EOF
export ENV_VAR=asdfasdf
export ENV_VAR2=asdfasdf
# etc
EOF

in an initialization process to set up the environment for further processes in the task.

Volumes and port mapping: I'm not sure aurora currently supports these; need to check the status of that.

I was able to figure out what went wrong. Apparently one of the Docker containers that we deployed was exposing two HTTP ports and this was leading to etcd keys being updated every second or so about a new port mapping for the containers. This basically meant that confd was updating nginx.conf every few seconds if not more frequently. Once we destroyed these apps and changed the associated Dockerfile to only use one port, the error/notice messages just went away from all routers! I just wanted to capture this here for anyone who stumbles across this issue.

```diff
+def remove_redundant_prefixes(prefix_list):
+    class Node:
+        def __init__(self, char):
+            self.char = char
+            self.children = {}
+
+    root = Node(None)
+
+    result_list = []
+    for prefix in sorted(prefix_list, key=lambda prefix: -len(prefix)):
+        cur = root
+        created = False
+        for c in prefix:
+            if c not in cur.children:
+                cur.children[c] = Node(c)
+                created = True
+            else:
+                created = False
+            cur = cur.children[c]
+
+        if created:
+            result_list.append(prefix)
+

After some experimentation yesterday, I think I've changed my stance on this, but it's still unclear what the path forward is.

#### Complication 1

Consider the following split.

- `Chain` object in process `A`
- Networking in process `B`
- JSON-RPC in process `C`

Separating `Chain` and networking keeps the networking processes from getting blocked by the `Chain` object.  Similarly, JSON-RPC needs it's own process to ensure similar guarantees to keep it from blocking the other processes.

The JSON-RPC server needs access to the `Chain` object to serve requests.  Naive approach would be to use the `multiprocessing` Manager pattern to serve it over a socket like we're currently doing with the databases.  This however doesn't work, because it doesn't allow us to serve JSON-RPC requests when we run an LES node because many of the JSON-RPC requests will require us to go out over the networking process to retrieve responses.

**Conclusion:** Whatever `Chain` object the JSON-RPC server uses must be able to delegate certain requests to the networking process.  This indicates that having the `Chain` and networking code combined as they are now might be the right choice.

conda doesn't really read pip metadata. `conda list` shells out to `pip list` and formats the result. That's the entirety of conda's interaction with pip. 

If you're using environment.yml, I'd say your workflow should be to edit that file and rerun `conda env` whenever you want to install a new package (or I think there might be a `conda env` command that does these both at once). Installing packages manually strictly speaking always puts the environment out of sync with the environment.yml (even if the environment itself is still self consistent).

@ukoethe, care to comment? I think you have some history with the VersionOrder spec.

According to the comments, this is very deliberate behavior. Text suffixes are treated as prerelease versions. The exception is the string "post".
```
    * Release versions contain only integers, e.g. '1.0', '2.3.5'.
    * Pre-release versions use additional letters such as 'a' or 'rc',
      for example '1.0a1', '1.2.beta3', '2.3.5rc3'.
    * Development versions are indicated by the string 'dev',
      for example '1.0dev42', '2.3.5.dev12'.
    * Post-release versions are indicated by the string 'post',
      for example '1.0post1', '2.3.5.post2'.
```
So what you want to do here is `2.1.1post.bioconda`:
```
>>> VersionOrder('2.1.1post.bioconda') < VersionOrder('2.1.1')
False
>>> VersionOrder('2.1.1post.bioconda') < VersionOrder('2.1.2')
True
```

Curator doesn't consider either to be old enough to be acted on.

Point of reference: `1476016158`, which is Sun, 09 Oct 2016 12:29:18 GMT. The point of reference is `unit_count` * `unit` * `# of seconds in a "unit"` subtracted from the epoch time at Curator run time.  

The times are measured exactly, and a strict greater than or less than marks the cutoff.  By that measure, those indices are not going to be caught by Curator, as is indicated in the log file:

`"***-***-en-2016-10-09" age (1476022761)` is Sun, 09 Oct 2016 14:19:21 GMT, which is almost 2 hours younger than the cutoff, so it doesn't get deleted.

`"***-***-en-2016-10-09" age (1476019759)` is Sun, 09 Oct 2016 13:29:19 GMT, which is one hour younger than the cutoff, so it doesn't get deleted.

> Tested on CLI and is returns only the Address Pool JSON

To clarify, are you commenting on the different between the HTTP response payload and the command output? The underlying command is a PUT on the load balancer, which will return the entire payload for the load balancer. However, since the command is for address pool create, the command will only return the portion of that object which reflects the newly created address pool.

We have tried to stay away for extra expansions inside of the CLI wherever we can since that makes it harder to understand the escaping/expansion that will yield the eventual value. As @brendandburns points out, the expansion in this case is normally done by the shell. The reason it doesn't happen here is because of the (optional) '=' between the option name and the value. If you drop that, it should expand as you expect (assuming that you are using bash that is :))

On the other hand Azure PowerShell CLI is optimized to be used in the PowerShell command line environment, under which it also supports tab completion. The essense of this issue, however, is to support tab completion for Azure CLI 2.0 (a bash-oriented tool) in PowerShell environment.

@jettify if you can provide feedback about usability and what you would like to have to improve the way tracing is handled will help us to improve the current design.

I'm still a bit reluctant about how the `trace_request_ctx` is handled, despite this PR reduces the verbosity of the signal handler is still necessary to pollute the caller, having something like `session.get(...., trace_request_ctx=value)`.

But this is IMHO a cosmetic discussion and it's up to the user who writes his own trace config used to trace his own requests to use the `trace_request_ctx` to share information that is only available in request time with the trace config handlers.

For third libraries, the intention is avoiding the use of this parameter. The user must be able to install, configure and forget it. The usage of the `trace_request_ctx` will create friction and will make more complex the maintainability of the code. Also worth mentioning that the use of this kw for more than one `TraceConfig` can be a nightmare.

So, how to pass information that is only available at request time for third-party libraries without polluting the `trace_request_ctx`? either wait for the context variables that will come with Python 3.7 or use an early implementation such as aiotask-context [1]

In any case, as I said I'm still a bit reluctant.  

[1] https://github.com/Skyscanner/aiotask-context

So, the fix is as following:
* make `request.app` normal property, not `reify`'ed attribute;
* make `request.app` return `match_info.current_app`
* add `match_info.current_app` property which, by default, returns last app (same as `request.app` did).
* add a context manager to match_info that updates `current_app`;
* update application's `_prepare_middleware` method to append one extra middleware
  `_fix_request_current_app` that switches current application in match info and, as a result, in request.

5.3.2.  absolute-form
   When making a request to a proxy, other than a CONNECT or server-wide
   OPTIONS request (as detailed below), a client MUST send the target
   URI in absolute-form as the request-target.

     absolute-form  = absolute-URI

   The proxy is requested to either service that request from a valid
   cache, if possible, or make the same request on the client's behalf
   to either the next inbound proxy server or directly to the origin
   server indicated by the request-target.  Requirements on such
   "forwarding" of messages are defined in Section 5.7.

   An example absolute-form of request-line would be:

     GET http://www.example.org/pub/WWW/TheProject.html HTTP/1.1

   To allow for transition to the absolute-form for all requests in some
   future version of HTTP, a server MUST accept the absolute-form in
   requests, even though HTTP/1.1 clients will only send them in
   requests to proxies.

Ah, hmm, this is tricky because I think this is traitlets behavior, e.g. with the following traitlets app:

```
import sys

from traitlets import Unicode
from traitlets.config import Application

class MyApp(Application):

    aliases = {'trait': 'MyApp.trait'}

    trait = Unicode("").tag(config=True)

    def start(self):
        super(MyApp, self).start()
        print(self.trait)
    

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app = MyApp()
    app.initialize(argv=sys.argv)
    app.start()
```

then you get the same error:

```
$ python test_traitlets.py --trait=1234
This is an application.

Options
-------

Arguments that take values are actually convenience aliases to full
Configurables, whose aliases are listed on the help line. For more information
on full configurables, see '--help-all'.

--trait=<Unicode> (MyApp.trait)
    Default: ''

To see all available configurables, use `--help-all`

[MyApp] CRITICAL | Bad config encountered during initialization:
[MyApp] CRITICAL | The 'trait' trait of a MyApp instance must be a unicode string, but a value of 1234 <class 'int'> was specified.
```

You can get around this by using escaped quotation marks:

```
$ python test_traitlets.py --trait="\"1234\""
1234
```

or using single and double quotes:

```
$ python test_traitlets.py --trait='"1234"'
1234
```

The full sentence:

> It's also better to default to too small, which tends to make cramped output, than too big, which can completely break things like tables.

If we guess a size smaller than the width that can be displayed, an ascii art table will be unnecessarily cramped, and may be split into two chunks, as in your screenshot. If we  guess a size *bigger* that the width that can be displayed, and it renders a table wider than can be displayed, the lines will wrap and the ascii art becomes a horrible mess. So where we don't know the width available to display monospace characters, we should err on the small side for picking a default. As we do.

[Send2Trash](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/Send2Trash) is a way to use the OS-provided trash mechanism, which should probably at least be an option, and possibly the default (since server admins are more likely to configure things like this than desktop users).

I don't think desktop 'recycle bins' are very well designed - as I described before, I think users tend to end up either bypassing them entirely, or accumulating thousands of 'deleted' files. @jdfreder no, this would be the relevant part:

>  'safe' (no sanitization to do) markdown is automatically trusted

Only the cells where sanitization would change the content would be left unrendered. Basically, all cells that work today would show up as rendered, just like they do now. But cells that include 'unsafe' content (as determined by caja) would be left unrendered until you decided to run them.

The only change with respect to current behavior is for these cells with untrusted content:

- today: the unsafe content is always stripped, but the safe remainder is still rendered.
- this pr: the unsafe content *may* be displayed, but won't be on load if the notebook is untrusted.

> Refreshing doesn't help

Try to force-refresh and clear cache. (usually Ctrl-shift-R a few times)

@tasdomas Sorry for the delayed response; sprints and whatnot.

I had already been talking with some of the other devs about these types of hooks, particularly in the context of `update-status` (see juju-solutions/layer-basic#100), and I think this PR is in the right general direction.  However, there are a couple of issues I have with the specifics.

First, I think we should move away from the base layer having implementation for things that represent fundamental aspects of how the Juju model works for charms, such as the fact that certain hooks have different types of context or are expected to perform different types of actions from "normal" hooks.  Thus, I would rather see the existing reactive main have this behavior folded in (as well as pulling in the other behavior currently in layer-basic, such as the aforementioned PR and the `config.X` flag management), rather than having a separate main with the base layer having to figure out which to call when.  That is to say, I would like to get back to the base layer being responsible solely for getting the reactive framework and charm code bootstrapped on the system in question.  (This will become more relevant in the future when we potentially have a different base layer for, e.g., Windows or Centos.)

Second, I **really** want to strongly deprecate `@hook`.  The usage of that decorator has led to significant issues with the ability to upgrade from non-reactive to reactive charms, and it also carries forward the potential for hook invocation order dependency errors in charms which was the main reason we created reactive style charms in the first place.  So, I'd like to extend the model that we use with the `config.changed` flag(s) to also handle cases like this (as well as `update-status` and the relation events).

I think everything you need for this is already present in Mesa.  Here's a little model I pulled together.  Its behavior isn't very interesting, but has the necessary attributes.

```python
import random
import itertools
from mesa import Model, Agent
from mesa.time import RandomActivation

unique_ids = itertools.count()

P_CHILD = 0.4
P_DEATH = 0.2

class Organism(Agent):
    def __init__(self, model, generation):
        super().__init__(next(unique_ids), model)
        self.generation = generation
        self.age = 0

    def step(self):
        if random.random() < P_CHILD:
            self.model.schedule.add(Organism(self.model, self.generation + 1))

        if random.random() < P_DEATH * self.age:
            self.model.schedule.remove(self)

        self.age += 1

class Population(Model):
    def __init__(self, seed=None):
        super().__init__(seed)
        self.schedule = RandomActivation(self)

        progenitor = Organism(self, 0)
        self.schedule.add(progenitor)

    def step(self):
        self.schedule.step()

model = Population()
for i in range(30):
    model.step()
    print(sorted([a.generation for a in model.schedule.agents]))
```

As @Corvince mentioned, you probably do not want to use different classes for Chlidren and Adults, as an instance can't change its class -- a child would have to always be a child.  If you wanted different behavior, you could use a conditional based on `self.age`.

The magic of the evolving population is in the `self.model.schedule.add` and `self.model.schedule.remove` calls, which add and remove agents from the population.

@erjac77 

The error for your modify method is because we need you to provide **kwargs , unlike update() method, modify, does not allow doing something like so:
```
node.description = 'testing this'
node.modify()
```

Correct way is:
```
node.modify(description='testing this'
```
The reason behind it is that modify is used to change a small number of parameters, usually 1 or 2, hence the need for **kwargs.

For this reason modify() method only sends the contents of **kwargs in the json payload. While update(), sends the entire __dict__ (minus _meta_data)

I hope this clarifies the difference.

I might speak with rest of the guys if we wanna change update() behavior to only accept **kwargs. However, I am against this change,  as update() uses HTTP PUT and it might lead to situations where if we have an existing object on bigip with some configured values, and we issue update() and not provide those values in the json payload the bigip might substitute this with its own internal defaults, leading to undesired configuration changes on the object.

There's a few things at play here which I agree makes this more difficult than it ideally could (should) be. For what it's worth, these are things we're actively looking to improve in .NET Core 3.0 and beyond, but let me try to explain the moving pieces as they are now.

As Peter said, there are two locations being flagged here by the scan for contain vulnerable pieces: the "runtime store", and the NuGet Fallback Folder. They're both used for different things at different times, but by themselves don't necessarily indicate that apps are using those versions and are thus vulnerable. Furthermore, how these things are used is different between .NET Core 1.x, 2.0, and 2.1 projects. It's different again if your ASP.NET Core application is targeting and thus running on .NET Framework, rather than on .NET Core.

Of these two locations, only the "runtime store" is tracked by installers, such that when you uninstall the files go away. The NuGet Fallback Folder is not removed when you uninstall .NET Core SDK versions and so older files will remain there until they're removed manually. The other important difference is that the "runtime store" is used by deployed applications *at runtime* (as they name implies), whereas the NuGet Fallback Folder is only used by projects during development. Once the project is published (and thus becomes an application) it either takes the assemblies it needs with it, or gets them from the "runtime store" (in 2.0) or a "shared framework" (in 2.1 and beyond).

In .NET Core 1.x and all .NET Framework projects, ASP.NET Core assemblies come from NuGet packages and are published with your application. The version you get is determined by the version of the package you reference in the project. So to ensure your project is up to date, you need to update the package versions referenced in the csproj file, then build/publish and redeploy it.

In .NET Core 2.0 projects, the ASP.NET Core assemblies come from NuGet packages at design/compile time, but typically *aren't* published with your application. Instead, at runtime they come from the "runtime store". However, the version the application needs is still dictated by the package version specified in the project file, so it's the same process for ensuring the project is up to date: update the package versions referenced in the csproj file, then build/publish and redeploy it. It also requires the additional step of ensuring the "runtime store" update is installed on the machines where the deployed application will actually run (i.e. the server).

In .NET Core 2.1 projects, the ASP.NET Core assemblies still come from NuGet packages at design/compile time, and they still aren't published with your application, but at runtime they come from a "shared framework" (located under C:\Program Files\dotnet\shared\Microsoft.AspNetCore.App). The key difference with this is that you don't need to change the version of ASP.NET Core in the project file, but rather you can leave it completely blank (e.g. <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.AspNetCore.App" />) . The build process will then transparently set that to the "baseline" version (currently 2.1.1) which is the same version you'll find in the NuGet Fallback Folder (so that the restore can complete without going online). However, at runtime, the application will roll-forward onto the latest patch version of the shared framework it finds on the machine (currently 2.1.2). This means that as patches are released, the NuGet Fallback Folder doesn't need new packages (as the baseline stays at 2.1.1), you just need to get a new shared framework (which of course is included in the updated SDKs). It also means on the servers, you simply need to install patched shared frameworks as they're released (2.1.2, 2.1.3, 2.1.4, etc.) and *all* apps on that server that were compiled against 2.1.0 will automatically roll forward onto the patched runtime when they next restart (rather than having to have their project files updated, build/published, and redeployed).

The end result of all this is that vulnerable assemblies will continue to be found in the NuGet Fallback Folder for the life of 2.x, but that doesn't mean they're necessarily used at runtime by your applications. Scanning of machines that install the SDK will continue to show these results, but scanning of servers (where you typically only install runtime assets) should be more inline with what one would expect.

I hope that helps.
Damian

Those issues are flagging Asp.net binaries in two different locations.

1. The runtime "store". This location is cumulative and things in here are used by applications based on exact version match. If anything in here is getting used, the application using it needs to be updated.

2. NugetFallbackFolder, this is a cache of nuget packages. Similar to above, the only way the version there will be used is if an application references it and in that case, the application needs to be updated.

objdump -T libcurl.so.4

00021aa8 g    DF .text	00000044  Base        curl_mfprintf

The issue is that Scrapy used Content-Type header to get the encoding ('utf-7'), while the site in fact seems to return utf-8. Then Scrapy decodes body using 'errors=replace' (w3lib_replace to be precise, see https://github.com/scrapy/w3lib/blob/34435d085c6adb14c94cd0188c23f6dc7d4da0f7/w3lib/encoding.py#L174) - and this produces an output which can't be encoded back to utf-8 for some reason.

I think the right place to fix it is probably w3lib. html-text can provide extra robustness by using surrogateescape, but it should be better to get a proper unicode body before passing it to html_text.

Working on this today. If we proceed with command line flags for `--pre-renewals-hook` and  `--post-renewals-hook` (I actually think we only need two hooks, not three: standalone users don't need to stop their servers unless something is actually being renewed), I think this might be the most usable of all the imperfect options:

* Use `shell=True`.  That allows people to supply `--post-renewals-hook "/etc/init.d/server reload"` or `--pre-renewals-hook "service nginx stop"` etc rather than having to write a script just because their command inherently requires arguments.
* Hackishly attempt to error out early if there is a PATH problem.  That can be accomplished by `split`ting the hook command and checking that the leftmost portion is in the PATH.  Though that can be wrong in crazy theoretical ways (commands whose names contain escaped spaces?) I think it's close enough to correct that it's safe to use.

A couple of recommendations for benchmarking:
 * Start measuring after the first successful delivery (or more) to avoid including startup costs in the measurement
 * Measure for at least 30s or more, anything shorter than that will be subject to ramp up costs.
 * Repeat the tests multiple times and use the highest/best achieved values as result.
 * Use histograms rather than averages whenever possible.
 * Make sure your run the clients on VM instances with at least 4 vcores/CPUs.
 * Increase throughput by promoting larger batches:
   * set `linger.ms=10`

Additionally, call poll(0) from your producer loop to quickly serve delivery reports.
Call flush(30) when terminating.

This is in fact not a typo. Just like `msg_container`, the `future_salts` response is using the bare type of the vector instead of the boxed type. This means that there is no `Vector` signature before the vector content, and the `future_salt` is also lower case (bare type, not boxed). So there is no `future_salt` signature before each salt.

`Vector<FutureSalt>` would be this:

    <VectorID><number of salts><FutureSaltID><salt><FutureSaltID><salt> ...

while `vector<future_salt>` is this:

    <number of salts><salt><salt> ...

Big difference, and the response from the server is just like the TL, the second version.

I'm unsure about how `validate` should behave.

Currently, by default, I kept current behaviour: it will raise `ValidationError` iff `strict is True`. And since `strict` is now always `True`, it always raises. Basically, it is now pretty much the same as load, but without the post_load methods:

```python
    def load(self, data, many=None, partial=None):
        return self._do_load(data, many, partial=partial, postprocess=True)

   def validate(self, data, many=None, partial=None):
        self._do_load(data, many, partial=partial, postprocess=False)
```

Maybe it would be more useful if it caught `ValidationError` and returned an error `dict` (which could be interpreted as a boolean by the caller).

You could do

```python
    if MySchema().validate(input_data)
```

or 

```python
    errors = MySchema().validate(input_data)
```

For instance, you'd do:

```python
    errors = MySchema().validate(input_data)
    if errors:
        ... # Manage errors
    else:
        ...  # Proceed
```
        
rather than

```python
    try:
        MySchema().validate(input_data)
    except ValidationError as exc:
        errors = exc.errors
        ...  # Manage errors
    else:
        ...  # Proceed
```

I favor try/except logics when the "normal" workflow does not trigger an exception, but if the user explicitly calls `validate` to check before acting, it makes sense to return a value rathen than raising an exception.

and comparatively the save/replace solution has problems:

1. save() is unfit for all uses given that it is unsafe. it should be fixed or removed from the API.

2. replace() is unfit for most uses given that its implementation is terribly inefficient most of the time.

keep in mind that the shortcoming of current save implementation is not a 'designed feature'. it is not a calculated compromise of performance and safety; it is a plain-old bug in the implementation, arising from not considering certain failure modes. its behavior is not something anyone would want to keep, but a bug that should be fixed.

fortunately the save() API is the right API for the fixed implementation (a safe enough save), so you can fix this without impacting clients.

- Yes, `(then ...)` is the same as `(and-then ...)`

- Yes, `(using-params ...)'  takes 1 tactic, 1 parameter name and 1 parameter value, in other words, it runs `simplify' with parameter `elim_and` set to `true`.

- The `bit-blast` tactic does not support everything, it relies on the simplifier to get rid of some functions first, that is why we have to run `simplify` just before `bit-blast`. The additional option `elim_and` is enabled to make sure the simplifier also rewrites and-expressions. 

- A "failure" is a proper error. The "fail-if-undecided" tactic which will report an error if a goal is not trivially say/unsat. Note that in general tactics do not "solve" a problem or "determine satisfiability", instead, they produce a set of goals from one input goal. Some tactics are guaranteed to always produce trivial result goals (or errors), but this is not true for all of them.

The restriction to use literals is a recurring pain. The reason is that formulas are generally pre-processed and therefore can be split or reassembled in arbitrary ways so we would not be able to track them as assumptions. One tradeoff is to allow arbitrary formulas, but forgo either that learned lemmas that are independent of the assumptions are retained or accumulate auxiliary formulas that introduce auxiliary literals on the side.

> p.s. since we're talking about layer redesign, is there any way to include a mechanic that quickly creates a duplicate of a layer with same shared params. This would be useful every time one has to use same embedding or convolution for different network inputs.

That sounds like something that should be doable, although it could get complicated because the `Layer` base class doesn't necessarily know what extra arguments are required to create a new instance of a given subclass. So instantiating a new instance in the usual way and then changing its parameter variables might not be straightforward.

> Possibly related: I thought that maybe this nonlinearity functionality could just be integrated into  `MultipleInputsLayer` (in `get_output`, I suppose)

Yep, that's what I meant. `MultipleInputsLayer` (and *every* layer, for that matter) could get a `nonlinearity=` keyword in its constructor so you don't need an extra `NonlinearityLayer` just like you don't need it after a `DenseLayer` already. The problem is that `nonlinearity=` currently defaults to `lasagne.nonlinearities.rectify` wherever it is defined. That wouldn't be a sensible default for `ConcatLayer`, for example, and it would also be awkward to have it default to the rectifier for some layer classes and to the identity for others. If we generally default it to identity, we could add `nonlinearity=` to the base `Layer` class such that all classes would transparently support it.

> So it does not make any breaks in the user code unless the user has own classes which extended `MergeLayer` as it has been removed. Anything else is back compatible (in theory).

From your description, you renamed `get_output_for` to `get_outputs_for` and added a default implementation of `get_output_for` in the base class. An existing `Layer` subclass will only overwrite `get_output_for`, and then it doesn't follow your new API. You'd need to provide a default implementation of `get_outputs_for` in the base class instead. Requiring single-output layers (which are the most common type) to return a tuple of tensors adds some unnecessary cognitive overhead. Only implementing `get_output_for` for single-output layers means they will end up with both `get_output_for` and `get_outputs_for` (and the same for the shapes), which at the very least would be mildly confusing to new users. So I think renaming these methods is not a good idea.

We... have a bit of a crazy monorepo, though it works well for us. We use mostly stock lerna for our setup. Within lerna terms, we have two different sets of packages, `packages` and `applications`. Everything in `packages` we ❤️ being able to edit along with the applications that use them, while also making sure that we transpile the versions we publish. This is the layout, just to help make some sense of it in here:

```
applications
├── commuter           ➡️ Next.js app, with an express server
├── desktop            ➡️ Electron app
├── jupyter-extension  ➡️ Python app with a webpack build
├── notebook-on-next   ➡️ Next.js app
├── play               ➡️ Next.js app
└── showcase           ➡️ Next.js app

packages
├── ansi-to-react
├── commutable
├── commuter-frontend
├── connected-components
├── core
├── display-area
├── dropdown-menu
├── editor
├── enchannel-zmq-backend
├── fs-observable
├── logos
├── markdown
├── mathjax
├── messaging
├── monaco-editor
├── notebook-app-component
├── notebook-preview
├── octicons
├── presentational-components
├── rx-binder
├── rx-jupyter
├── styles
├── timeago
├── transform-dataresource
├── transform-geojson
├── transform-model-debug
├── transform-plotly
├── transform-vdom
├── transform-vega
├── transforms
├── transforms-full
├── types
├── vega-embed2
└── webpack-configurator  ➡️ Common webpack configuration to allow apps to build
```

As you noted in https://github.com/zeit/next.js/pull/4417:

> My main concern however is that your logic there relies on the using having configured a .babelrc, since your logic on its own will compile those modules, but not enable any plugins/presets on its own.

We have an individual `.babelrc` for the lerna root that mostly gets used by the packages (and the jupyter extension and then a separate config for the next.js apps.

I get the control flow graph of a function using CFGFast.  But the CFG may contain the start address of a called function. So I want to ignore such an address and only preserve the edges within the current analyzed function. As a result, I don't need to know the accurate end address.  The address of the last instruction works well. I don't know how to get the size of the last instruction of a block.

Fantastic... this could have been triggered by literally any function using the simprocedure continuations on ARM, given that the function actually returned. The problem is that we're not correctly saving the lr register, so when we return, we pull the address to return to from lr, but it's been overwritten by the calling function. So basically we need to save the appropriate caller-save registers before making a call out. I think this should only be the link registers, since simprocedures should never actually touch any general purpose registers, so I'm going to forgo implementing this in the calling convention as a "correct" caller-save implementation.

The `VFG` is indeed a self-made term. It stands for _Value Flow Graph_, which encodes the analysis result from Value-Set Analysis (a type of static analysis that `angr` does, usually referred to as `VSA`) into a graph and link those states together according to the corresponding CFG. VSA yields a better analysis result than CFG for many reasons, and you may want to check it out from other papers if you have never heard of it before.

Note that in theory VSA is a sound approximation (or in another term, a sound analysis), but it is not sound in our implementation for various reasons, mostly for performance concerns. Feel free to make it sound if that is important to you, but soundness is not in our plan for now.

I also agree that this project has sort of fallen into disrepair. A few weeks ago my coworker @ehebert also expressed this concern. After that conversation we reached out to Anaconda to get admin access to the blaze organization which allows me to add new commiters. I know that you have been working with and on odo for a long time so I would be happy to give you commit access.

One issue I see with odo in general is that, by design, we consume a huge set of packages. These packages are all constantly changing and breaking odo. In expectation, no single odo developer uses or cares about the vast majority of edges in odo. I know that this has contributed to my lack of enthusiasm for odo because it is hard to invest a lot of time maintaining code you never intend to use while trying not to break someone's production systems. I think odo is a project that would be best served with a large number of contributors who each need to think about just a small set of edges. > As the code stands in this PR, it changes the localDir then call get and that's it. I would say it should at least revert localDir back given the above. However, I would also argue that it should call get (now defined as making sure the file is in localDir) and then do a local file copy thus not 'abusing' get 

There are a few issues here.

- Ganga should not change attributes that the user is setting directly, not even temporary. So we 
  should  *not* modify `localDir`.
- I do not think that we should call `get` first. The `IGangaFile` object will often be readonly for the 
  user and might point to a place with no disk space available. This would be *really* annoying for the 
  user.

>  It's possible @milliams feels get should call copyTo but that would involve shifting the actual file retrieval code to copyTo instead.

@agrunnet Perhaps I should ask this in a separate question, but how do I ensure that all realsense postprocessing disabled? We're attempting to integrate simultaneous cameras, but the stream has slowed down to a crawl. We have 3 cameras on 2 USB controllers. We were expecting 1 fast stream and 2 slow ones, but we got 3 _very_ slow ones. We also noticed our CPU was being maxed out (400%).

I loaded custom configs that I created with one of the realsense tools, but I specifically disabled all of the postprocessing since we have our own engine that does the filtering we want. In spite of that, we less than 1 fps on all cameras and maxed out cpu usage. I disabled our filtering altogether and it didn't help very much; the pipe.wait_for_frame() is what's taking so long.

Our configs (hand copied, so some typos may be present):
```
{
"controls-autoexposure-auto": "False",
"controls-autoexposure-auto": "133660",
"controls-depth-gain": "16", //Why can't this go lower?
"controls laserpower": "180",
"controls laserstate": "on",
"ignoreSAD": 0,
"stream-depth-format": "Z16",
"stream-fps": "15", //this is nowhere close to what we're seeing
"stream-height": "720",
"stream-width": "1280"
}
```

Issue solved! I changed the `dev->wait_for_frames()` to `dev->poll_for_frames()` . The functionality of `wait_for_frames()` blocks until new frames are arrived while 'poll_for_frames()` checks if new frames are arrived without blocking! So, the second method can be used for maximum FPS (I have encountered even 200 FPS)

Hi! This looks like a really good start, I personally would be keen to see something like this merge. A couple of notes:

Personally, I tend to prefer the prefetch in most cases. It should certainly be possible to override this behaviour on certain FK links, as the joins can become quite expensive. [My version](https://gist.github.com/mjtamlyn/e8e03d78764552289ea4e2155c554deb) of this problem ignored select related completely.

I've found it necessary to be able to apply more complex optimisations than just a simple `prefetch_related`, by using a `Prefetch` object. It's also quite common for custom fields on a child object to depend on an attribute of the parent. I think you've touched on that with the `Meta.optimizations`, but I think that probable needs a more flexible API. I came up with an API of a `classmethod` called `optimize_<field_name>(cls, queryset, context)`. This also allows you to apply optimizations only for certain user types for example - it doesn't matter whether the request asks for a "liked this post" field if the user is anonymous we know the answer is false and don't need to optimise it. This could actually be a more general API that isn't so dependent on the ORM or Django.

My project is starting to grow some microservices, and I recently had cause to add a `post_process_<field>` hook alongside the preprocessing `optimize_<field>` hook, so that prefetch_related-style optimizations can be applied over the microservice boundary. Run a bulk request to the service after the queryset has loaded, and annotate the queryset objects with the relevant information. This is probably out of scope for this particular project, but I though it's interesting to mention.

I'm concerned about your field extraction - I have a suspicion that it won't handle fragments correctly. For users of Relay, almost every request will include an extensive number of fragments, so extracting the actual requested fields from these is important.

I know the documentation now recommends a `data-loader` clone style approach using promises and lazy resolution. To me, this isn't a very "Django-flavoured" approach to the N+1 problem, and I've not had the time to experiment with it to see if it works. I would be quite interested in Syrus' thoughts.

This should be now fixed in `master` :)

1. Move ` url(r'^silk/', include('silk.urls', namespace='silk'))` up to around line 30, so it is always in your url urlpatterns **or**
2. Set `DEBUG = False` when running your migration scripts

I think there are two remaining issues:

1. Usability. The template panel becomes less useful if you have to wade through hundreds or even thousands of widget templates to find the one non-widget template you're looking for. (On the other hand, not being able to see what widget templates are rendering at all could cause its own problems.)
2. Performance. The merged fix improves this dramatically, so the remaining issue is probably the large response sizes (potentially >100 MB) that can happen when template context is shown.

Your suggestion of a setting to suppress widget-related templates might make sense.

Another option (which might overcomplicate things) could be to do something like the SQL panel, where it detects duplicate templates or contexts (I think the merged fix already detects the latter?) and doesn't bother to render them, instead noting by the template path that it was duplicated X times. Perhaps you could click to have it AJAX load them in anyway. (Is that still even possible, once the page has rendered?) This way, you could reduce response size by quite a bit for certain cases.

Alternatively, if it is possible to retrieve template contexts once the page has already rendered, perhaps contexts don't load until you click on them.

Yet another option could be to just suppress repetitive templates related to select widgets. In my case, that's django/forms/widgets/select_option.html and django/forms/widgets/attrs.html.

What about allowing a set of filters for templates to exclude from the templates panel? Ex: "django/forms/widgets/*". I don't know how you'd determine that a template is part of a widget, and if you have to hard-code such a filter, it might as well be user-configurable.

@fortran-favors-the-old What is your pip version?  It looks like problem 2 that you identified is addressed in the most recent versions of pip (9.0.0+) by the PEP 503 support here:
https://github.com/pypa/pip/commit/c2101982cb5b28963fe22792cbb30ce50988ab8d

Namely, the newer pip should not try to download and install a version of a package known to be incompatible with the current python version if other versions of that package exist that are compatible.  On my machine with pip 9.0.1 this manifests itself as reliably choosing futures 3.1.1 over 3.2.0.

To be clear, I'm not disputing that we should still ensure this works for older pip versions and that issue 1 is a problem in its own right, but just confirming my understanding of the issue.

Thanks for the report.  I wonder if the URL issue might be a red herring - PR #265 which added support for passing windows drive names as logdirs without having the drive interpreted as a run name.  So in 1.5.0 `--logdir=C:\tmp` will result in a run using the directory `C:\tmp` with the default run name `.`, while in 0.4.0 `--logdir=C:\tmp` would result in a run using the directory `\tmp` on the current drive, with the run renamed to `C`.  The 0.4.0 filename's `run=D%5C.png` would correspond to a run named `D\` which looks a lot like a drive name, maybe - were you running TensorBoard from `D` drive?

> or

@mgrand 

> You mention that when computing the hash for an ID, non-ascii characters should not be escaped. What should be done with them?

A few steps to follow, when hashing the ID:

1. Ensure that the JSON serializer you're using outputs a string in some encoding, e.g. UTF-8, UTF-16, etc. It most likely does.
1. Convert the JSON string into a binary representation for your SHA3 lib (make sure it's FIPS202 compliant!). **You may not have to do this if your SHA3 lib does this for you (if it just takes a normal string type as an input, you can probably skip this step**.
1. Digest the created hash into a hexadecimal string (in whichever encoding format, most likely UTF-16 for Java?)

In Python, we do the following (note that Python uses UTF-8 strings internally):

```python
json_str_tx = '...'  # UTF-8 JSON string
bin_str_tx = json_str_tx.encode()  # encode the UTF-8 string into a binary representation
txid = sha3.sha3_256(bin_str_tx).hexdigest()  # create the hash and digest it into a hexadecimal (UTF-8) string
```

> I've implemented a simple proof-of-concept example which rejects reference casts for untracked instances, but accepts them by value

This is a pretty harsh restriction: it means I can't pass a python instance to a function that takes a `const Type &`, which is probably the vast majority of functions/methods out there that take non-primitive, read-only arguments.  It's also sort of hitting a nail with a sledgehammer: the problem isn't really with accessing references from C++, it's with returning references back to Python.

To summarize, just to be sure I understand the issues correctly (correct me if I have anything wrong here), the main issue here is that the following will result in a double-free:

```C++
m.def("get", [&]() { return new SomeType(); }, py::return_value_policy::copy_untracked);
m.def("pass_through", [&](SomeType &s) { return &s; })
```
```Python
a = get()
b = pass_through(a)
```
because the `pass_through` return value caster doesn't know about the internal C++ instance associated with `a`, and so it takes over ownership.  Without `copy_untracked` on `get`, this wouldn't be a problem: the caster would see the already-registered instance and just increment the reference count on it.  (This also means `a is b` is true without copy_untracked, but false with it).

Another couple of things to think about on this topic:

- It would be nice to be able to override the built-in casters with a custom type caster.  For example, someone asked in the gitter channel about binding code that expects `std::string` in a non-UTF-8 encoding.  Since 2.1, this is actually possible: you can declare a `template <> type_caster<std::string>{ ... }` which will get precedence (as a full specialiation) over the built-in string caster (a `std::basic_string` partial specialization).  It might be possible to take this a step further by having something like: `template <Type, SFINAE = void> class custom_caster : public builtin_caster<Type> {}`, so that custom casters can indeed override builtin casters.

- `load()` would be nicer if it accepted an `load_options` struct rather than `convert`; the struct would itself contain `convert`, but could also contain other values.  This would make adding features into the cast/load system much easier in the future (existing casters would not need any changes, while maintaining the same API).  For example, the `py::arg().none()` feature could be extended to allow custom casters to optionally accept `None` through this.  `cast()`, to be consistent, could similarly wrap the current `return_value_policy` and `parent` arguments into a `cast_options` struct.

Actually, there's one more thing.

Currently, we call `PyArray_FromAny` in the ctor (in `ensure_array`) and it sort of makes sense for the cases when you are lazy and want to pass something array-like or just a scalar and let numpy handle it for you. This is more of a nice-to-have kind of thing, but still.

Now, when we're converting an unknown object into an array, there's two options -- we don't have a dtype, just the flags (`array`), or we do (`array_t<>`). Then there's two more options -- the input is already an array, or it's not.

The way `PyArray_FromAny` handles casting is: if "forcecast" flag is not set (which it wouldn't normally be), the casting is set to "safe". However, this would prohibit e.g. accepting int64 arrays when int32 is required. Here's a short illustration of casting rules:

```python
>>> import numpy as np
>>> import pandas as pd
>>> casting = 'no', 'equiv', 'safe', 'same_kind', 'unsafe'
>>> types = 'int32', 'int64', 'double'
>>> pd.DataFrame([(f, t, c, np.can_cast(f, t, c)) 
...     for f in types for t in types for c in casting if f != t],
...     columns=['from', 'to', 'casting', 'can_cast']).set_index(['from', 'to', 'casting'])
                        can_cast
from   to     casting           
int32  int64  no           False
              equiv        False
              safe          True
              same_kind     True
              unsafe        True
       double no           False
              equiv        False
              safe          True
              same_kind     True
              unsafe        True
int64  int32  no           False
              equiv        False
              safe         False
              same_kind     True
              unsafe        True
       double no           False
              equiv        False
              safe          True
              same_kind     True
              unsafe        True
double int32  no           False
              equiv        False
              safe         False
              same_kind    False
              unsafe        True
       int64  no           False
              equiv        False
              safe         False
              same_kind    False
              unsafe        True
```

It would be nice to be able to relax casting rules down to "same_kind" or tighten it to "equiv" for ctors / type casters, but I'm not quite sure how it should be expressed. `PyArray_FromAny` doesn't directly accept casting policy, so it should be done separately / manually. There's `PyArray_CanCastTo` which accepts two dtypes and performs the check and `PyArray_CastTo` which does the actual casting.

I thought of something like this:

1) casting an object to `array`: don't have target dtype, do nothing
2) constructing an `array` or `array_t` from a pointer: the dtypes will always match, so do nothing
3) casting an object to `array_t`, input is already an array: check if can cast, do the cast
4) casting an object to `array_t`, input is not an array: either
4.a) convert it to an array without specifying target dtype (so that minimal dtype is inferred), then 3)
4.b) convert it to an array with target dtype (in which case it's almost like a forcecast), then do nothing

Also, it's not clear where these casting policy should go. It's not really a flag, more like an enum, and there's already flags in the type signature (which I'm not too fond of either). Also, I think it matters mostly for the function signatures (both the input / output types), because in other parts of code you're always free to do `.astype(whatever, casting_policy="...")`.

So after thinking about this a bit more, I don't think this is worth doing. The problem is that in 0.7.0, we started having users check the `certbot` version on PyPI instead of the `letsencrypt` version. This version check is used to determine whether or not `certbot-auto` should update itself and the Python packages.

Suppose we made 0.8.0 the last release to use `letsencrypt-auto-source` and then we release 0.9.0. There are likely users who had updated to 0.7.0, but not 0.8.0, meaning that `certbot-auto` will look for a new version itself in `letsencrypt-auto-source` on the 0.9.0 tag which will not exist.

If we didn't point people to `certbot` PyPI packages in 0.7.0, we could have theoretically made this change, however, we'd have to make sure that `letsencrypt-auto-source` existed in the tag matching every shim release. Would we have remembered to do this?

There are workarounds (such as creating `letsencrypt-auto-source` only during the release process), but I don't think it's worth the effort so I'm closing this issue.

@jonnybarnes Not previously, but I'll summarize it here:

Right now, we store four files in `/etc/letsencrypt/archive/example.com/`: `certN.pem`, `chainN.pem`, `fullchainN.pem`, `privkeyN.pem`, where N increases over time. We also symlink the most recent of each of these files into `/etc/letsencrypt/live/example.com`, with the N removed, so server software can be configured with a reliable filename. This symlinking step is considered "atomic"[1], so that all four files in `live/` match each other at any given time.

The question is: If you have identical certificates for multiple key types, should we store them in separate lineages (i.e. `archive/example.com` for RSA certs and `archive/example.com-ecdsa` for ECDSA certs), or should we consider them part of the same lineage, adding new filenames to the collection (i.e. `archive/example.com/ecdsa-certN.pem`, `archive/example.com/ecdsa-privkeyN.pem`).

Mostly this comes down to a question of what we want to do if renewal fails for one key type, but succeeds for the other. Do we consider both renewals to have failed, and retry both later? Or do take the successful one and configure it, and retry only the failed one later? The former implies the "same lineage" approach. The latter implies the "separate lineages" approach. I think we are slowly arriving at consensus on the "separate lineages" approach. Is that your feeling too, @bmw, @ohemorange, @ynasser?

[1] Note that the symlinking is not actually atomic in the Unix operation sense, and a poorly-timed crash could result in an inconsistent state, but that is a totally different issue.

By the way, your model has an issue: you have no guarantee that ``country.capital_city.country == country``.
I'd change your model to:

```python
class City(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    capital_of = models.OneToOneField(
        Country,
        related_name='capital_city',
        blank=True, null=True, on_delete=models.CASCADE,
    )

class Country(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=200)
    # Django will provide each Country instance with a magical `capital_city` attribute
```

With those models, the ``CountryFactory`` would create a ``City`` pointing (through its one-to-one field) to the ``Country`` instance; Django's related field feature would then allow you to query ``country.capital_city`` and fetch that city.

OK, I think i got it.

With:
```python
@factory.lazy_attribute
def school(self):
     return foo
```

When building or creating an object, we'll end up with ``.create(school=foo)`` (``foo`` being the value returned by this call).
Since you return a ``factory.Iterator``, this is equivalent to calling ``Department(school=factory.Iterator(...))`` — SQLAlchemy doesn't know what to do with that ``factory.Iterator``.

You should simply replace that block with:

```python
school = factory.Iterator(Session.query(models.School).all())
```

The problem lies in Django https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/contrib/auth/__init__.py#L184 because if you (like me) left path `social.backends...` instead of `social_core.backends...` in `AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS` then you are correctly authenticated but `AuthenticationMiddleware` did not detect it.

```
# doesn't work
AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS = (
    'social.backends.facebook.FacebookOAuth2',
    ...
)

# works
AUTHENTICATION_BACKENDS = (
    'social_core.backends.facebook.FacebookOAuth2',
    ...
)
```

Having two command line arguments that do the same thing is generally not a good idea. I would say that `http-header` is superior to `http-headers`. With `http-header` you can specify multiple Headers in different places, for example in `streamlinkrc` and in the command line, with those on the command line taking precedence over those in the config. `http-headers` will overwrite all of the headers and cannot be combined from the config file. 

You can load configuration options for a file too. For example, for the stream you give in your example you could create `stream.cfg`:
```conf
http-header=User-Agent=android
http-header=X-Forwarded-For=0.0.0.0
http-header=X-Requested-With=
http-header=Referer=
http-header=X-Do-Not-Track=
http-cookie=authToken=4545s4fsfsf4s
http-query-param=video_id=100
http-query-param=authority_instance_id=spectar-prd-cgt
http-query-param=token=74n8mnSREdQ4X-anI1rCXw
```
And then load it on the command line using the `@` syntax (which might not be documented):
```shell
$ streamlink @stream.cfg "hlsvariant://http://test.se/example" 
```
And then, if you wish to override the cookie or one of the query params you can just add those on the command line too:
```shell
$ streamlink @stream.cfg --http-cookie "authToken=aNewToken" --http-query-param "token=otherNewToken" "hlsvariant://http://test.se/example" 
```

Much cleaner, and more manageable I think...
